1. To calculate the probability that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A, we need to find $ P(X_B > X_A) $. This is equivalent to finding the probability that a random variable from a normal distribution with mean $ \mu_B = 75 $ and standard deviation $ \sigma_B = 12 $ is greater than a random variable from a normal distribution with mean $ \mu_A = 70 $ and standard deviation $ \sigma_A = 10 $.

We can calculate this probability by finding the difference between the two normal distributions and then finding the probability that this difference is positive. The difference between the two normal variables is also normally distributed with mean $ \mu_{B - A} = \mu_B - \mu_A = 75 - 70 = 5 $ and standard deviation $ \sigma_{B - A} = \sqrt{\sigma_B^2 + \sigma_A^2} = \sqrt{12^2 + 10^2} = \sqrt{144 + 100} = \sqrt{244} \approx 15.62 $.

To find the probability that $ X_B > X_A $, we can standardize the difference and use the standard normal distribution. The z-score for the difference is $ z = \frac{0 - 5}{15.62} \approx -0.32 $. The probability that a standard normal variable is greater than this z-score is the area to the right of $ z = -0.32 $, which is $ 1 - \Phi(-0.32) $, where $ \Phi $ is the cumulative distribution function of the standard normal distribution.

Using a standard normal table or a calculator, we find that $ \Phi(-0.32) \approx 0.3783 $, so the probability that $ X_B > X_A $ is $ 1 - 0.3783 = 0.6217 $.

This means that there is approximately a 62.17% chance that a randomly selected student from Group B has a higher engagement level than a randomly selected student from Group A.
